design_philosophy.core_loop: Player acts as CEO managing airline operations: purchase airport slots, establish routes, manage aircraft fleet, set pricing and operations, compete for regional dominance while responding to historical and random events over 20-year campaign
design_philosophy.primary_tension: Competition between 4 airlines (AI or players) for regional market dominance; resource scarcity (airport slots, aircraft availability, capital); geopolitical event disruption; competing objectives requiring balanced strategic decisions
design_philosophy.player_fantasy: Build and manage a global airline empire from startup to industry dominance; navigate real and alternate historical scenarios; outmaneuver competitors through smart acquisition and operational strategy
design_philosophy.what_makes_it_opinionated: Deterministic win condition that allows zero draws and zero stalemates—only one airline can win; loss condition if no airline meets objectives; forces aggressive competitive positioning rather than peaceful coexistence; geopolitical events embedded as core simulation mechanics that directly impact airline viability and aircraft availability

difficulty.player_agency_in_difficulty: Multiple difficulty settings available via era selection (1955–1975, 1970–1990, 1985–2005, 2000–2020); winning condition thresholds vary by difficulty (4–7 regions required); player chooses number of AI competitors (implied); no rewind mechanics—performance evaluated annually with cumulative multi-year objectives

progression.upgrade_system: Aircraft procurement system: access to different aircraft types depends on geopolitical relations and era; Eastern Bloc airlines have cheaper but less efficient aircraft until 1986 (perestroika); Western airlines have access to Boeing/McDonnell-Douglas/Lockheed from start; pricing/availability shift with historical events
progression.unlock_mechanism: Geopolitical events unlock/lock aircraft options and trading partners dynamically (e.g., post-1986 Eastern Bloc gains US aircraft access); regions become accessible as airlines progress; new historical eras unlock different aircraft and map conditions
replayability_hooks: 4 different historical eras with distinct geopolitical contexts and aircraft technology
Random civil wars, natural disasters, and hypothetical events create varied campaign trajectories
Multiplayer competitive mode allows different strategic approaches
20-year campaign window with annual evaluation cycles encourages strategic experimentation

world_building_techniques: Historical event simulation (Olympics, wars, economic crises, geopolitical shifts) embedded as mechanical consequences affecting aircraft availability and market conditions
Geopolitical faction modeling: Eastern Bloc vs. Western Bloc relations mechanically restrict aircraft procurement and market access until Cold War resolution (1986–1991)
Era-based world states: 1955–1975 (jet dawn), 1970–1990 (oil crises/Cold War end), 1985–2005 (economic prosperity), 2000–2020 (supersonic aircraft, alternate EU expansion, climate change events)
Hypothetical future scenario (2000–2020) explores speculative geopolitical outcomes (Russia/Belarus/Ukraine/Switzerland EU membership, supersonic aircraft obsolescence, fossil fuel crises)

faction_indicators: Eastern Bloc airlines (constrained aircraft access, cheaper Asian/African/Middle Eastern relations until 1986)
Western Bloc airlines (early access to premium US/European aircraft)
Regional hubs required to establish dominance in every world region
danger_signals: Cold War tensions restrict aircraft acquisition and trading partners for Eastern airlines (1955–1986)
Oil crises (1973) and economic instability eras penalize inefficient aircraft
Random civil wars disrupt regional operations at unpredictable intervals
Mandatory competitive elimination: stalemates impossible; only one airline wins
environmental_storytelling: Geopolitical map reflects real and alternate historical outcomes (Berlin Wall fall, Hong Kong retrocession, EU expansion)
Aircraft availability/pricing tied to real manufacturing nations and trade relationships
Natural disasters (New Zealand volcanic eruption, Thailand floods, São Paulo earthquake) represent climate/environmental change in late-game scenarios
Cold War era features restricted access zones and geopolitical alignment mechanics
